title: BreathSign: Transparent and Continuous In-ear Authentication Using Bone-conducted Breathing Biometrics

method.input_representation: MFCCs, Sub-band Energy Ratio (SER), Pearson correlation coefficients
method.training_strategy: Metric learning using Triplet Loss to reconstruct behavior-independent features

evaluation_protocol: 15 legitimate users, 5 spoofers; 20% training, 80% testing for legitimate users

reported_limitations: Performance drops significantly during strenuous exercise (9km/h)
Interference from sound playing in earphones (music)
Potential impact of respiratory diseases on biometric features

usability.usability_issues: Requires the user to wear earphones; performance degraded by intense physical activity

key_contribution: Proposed BreathSign, using bone-conducted breathing sounds via inward-facing earphone microphones for continuous authentication
Designed a Triplet network to remove the impact of breathing behavior (fast, normal, deep) on biometric features
main_strength: Robustness to earphone wearing positions and daily environments
Strong resistance to air-conduction and bone-conduction replay attacks
main_weakness: Lack of evaluation while audio is playing in earphones
insights.notes_for_review: The paper reports 'Authentication Accuracy' and 'Spoofing Detection Rate' instead of EER/FAR/FRR. It demonstrates high temporal stability over 32 days.
